policy_statement: Priority Action #2: Sustainable and Green Boats to convert tourist cruise boats from diesel to LPG or CNG/NG, with project lifetime Dec 2017–Dec 2020.
mechanism_description: The project converts tourist cruise boats from diesel to LPG or CNG/natural gas, lowering the emissions intensity of boat operations and reducing transport-related greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Fuel choice in transport → ↓ fuel carbon intensity (and combustion CO₂e) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Marine/tourist cruise boat operations shift from diesel engines/fueling systems to LPG or CNG/natural-gas fueling (often set engine conversion/retrofit or replacement and new bunkering logistics).  
2) Mediating process: Lower carbon content per unit energy and different combustion characteristics reduce CO₂ emissions per kWh of propulsion; controls can also affect methane slip and black carbon formation.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions from vessel operation (and potentially reduced black carbon), decreasing transport-related GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing lifecycle GHG emissions from marine transport by lowering the carbon intensity of the fuel burned (and associated combustion emissions).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Fuel switching lowers the emissions factor of marine propulsion, directly reducing operational (and potentially lifecycle) GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there may be co-benefits (air quality, noise, possibly efficiency), the defining and largest climate effect across policies using this mechanism is direct emissions reduction from lower-carbon fuel combustion.  
3) Edge cases applied: If natural gas systems have high methane leakage/slip, net climate benefit could be reduced or negated; however, the mechanism intent and typical dominant pathway remains mitigation via lower fuel carbon intensity, assuming standard control practices.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that switches marine fuels from higher- to lower-carbon options is fundamentally aimed at reducing GHG emissions from transport operations, so “Mitigation” holds across the mechanism class.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether this is best characterized as “fuel switching,” “alternative fuels,” “fleet modernization,” or “port/harbor decarbonization” is policy-evidence dependent and should be assigned at the individual policy level, not at the mechanism-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Natural gas (CNG/NG) can have methane slip and upstream leakage that may erode climate benefits; lifecycle accounting and slip-control requirements can materially affect net mitigation magnitude, but the primary intent/effect remains emissions reduction via lower-carbon fuel switching.
key_indicators: “convert tourist cruise boats from diesel to LPG or CNG/NG”; “consists mainly in converting all these boats”; “operate with LPG … or CNG”
co_benefits: Air-quality improvements (lower SOx/PM/black carbon) and potential public health/noise benefits, but not framed as primary objectives in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: The text does not specify whether conversion is funded/subsidized (incentive program) versus required (mandate). Classified as an incentive/programmatic intervention because it is presented as a time-bound “project” with a defined lifetime, not as a legal requirement.
